summary:
The Kingdom of Kongo (c. 1390–1914 AD) was the most powerful and sophisticated pre-colonial state in Central Africa, centered on the lower Congo River basin (spanning modern DRC, Republic of Congo, and Angola), and notable for its early voluntary embrace of Christianity, its complex administrative system, and its ultimately tragic engagement with the [[event:2341|Atlantic slave trade]] that undermined its society from within. At its 15th–16th century peak, the kingdom governed an estimated 2–3 million people through a system of provincial governors appointed by the Manikongo (king) and a sophisticated bureaucracy that maintained roads, markets, and internal order. Portuguese contact beginning in 1483 brought early diplomatic relations of unusual equality: King Afonso I (r. 1509–1543) converted sincerely to Christianity, built churches, educated Kongolese priests, and conducted diplomatic correspondence with European monarchs as a sovereign peer.

Afonso I's famous letters to the King of Portugal (1526) documented the devastating impact of the slave trade on Kongo society, as Portuguese and allied traders raided villages and corrupted Kongolese nobles to supply enslaved people: 'We cannot reckon how great the damage is, since the mentioned merchants are taking every day our natives, sons of the land and sons of our noblemen and vassals and our relatives.' Despite his protests, the trade continued and intensified. The Kingdom of Kongo fragmented after the Battle of Mbwila (1665), in which Portuguese-backed forces from Angola defeated and killed the Manikongo, shattering central authority. The subsequent civil wars, combined with the continued slave trade, devastated the region over the next two centuries. The kingdom nominally survived until Portuguese colonization formalized in the late 19th century.
